prompt: 2026 USER EMPATHY on the LEAD TIME of security audits / reviews / pentests. A software company (often shipping AI) must pass a security audit or review to close an enterprise deal. The traditional path has lead time: schedule an external audit/pentest firm, wait weeks or months, expensive, lots of back-and-forth, and you get a static PDF the buyer has to take on trust. Find real 2026 posts/threads (@handle/source + date, verbatim) where founders/engineers/security people complain about: how LONG a security audit or pentest takes, scheduling/waitlists, the cost, the lead time blocking a deal, wanting it faster / on-demand / continuous / self-serve. How do they describe the wait and the pain in their own words? Does an ON-DEMAND, instant, repeatable security audit (vs a weeks-long engagement) resonate as a strong value prop? List the 10 most resonant verbatim phrases about audit/pentest lead time and the desire for something faster.

NOUN CHOICE for a SECURITY product, 2026. kolm runs an automated, deterministic, self-serve examination of the AI a company ships (permissions, data egress, prompt-injection resistance, audit-trail integrity, etc.) and outputs a SIGNED, independently verifiable report. We want to call the OUTPUT/ACTION the most relatable + accurate noun. Compare: 'security audit', 'security review', 'security assessment', 'security attestation', 'security evidence', 'security report', 'security scan'. Which noun (a) makes a buyer instantly understand + want it, (b) is accurate without overclaiming (e.g. does 'audit' wrongly imply an accredited/CPA/third-party SOC 2 audit? does 'attestation' sound formal/legal?), and (c) is searchable. Cite how 2026 security/devtools products name this (Vanta, pentest-as-a-service like Cobalt/HackerOne, scanners like Snyk/Wiz, attestation/Sigstore, etc., with URLs). Recommend the single best primary noun + when to use the alternates. The founder who shipped the product strongly prefers 'audit' - validate or push back, and if 'audit' is right, how to use it accurately.

POSITIONING FRAME TEST, 2026. A founder said the relatable opener is: 'You shipped your product, and every enterprise deal needs a security audit.' Then the product (kolm) is 'on demand, reproducible, verifiable, end-to-end security audits without the lead time.' Evaluate: is anchoring on 'your product' (the thing the founder built and identifies with) + 'every enterprise deal needs a security audit' the right relatable situation? Is 'your product' better than 'your AI' / 'your AI agent' as the anchor for a founder, given kolm specifically audits the AI/agent part (from logs) and is AI-specific? How do we stay accurate (kolm audits the AI/agent, not a full-product pentest) while anchoring relatably on the product + the deal + the audit? Cite real 2026 founder language about 'every enterprise deal needs X' and security audits blocking deals. Recommend the exact relatable framing: what to anchor on, and how to bridge from 'your product / your deal' to 'we audit your AI on demand'.

COPY OPTIMIZATION. Optimize this founder's raw value statement into a great homepage hero: 'On demand, reproducible, verifiable end-to-end security audits without the lead time.' Context: kolm runs an instant, deterministic, self-serve security audit of the AI a company ships and outputs a signed report the buyer verifies themselves, so enterprise deals clear their security review in days not weeks (vs hiring a pentest/audit firm and waiting weeks for an unverifiable PDF). Keep the power words that matter (on demand, reproducible, verifiable, no lead time / no wait) but make it human and sharp. Decide what to LEAD with: the speed/on-demand (instant vs weeks), the verifiability (buyer checks it themselves), or the situation (every enterprise deal needs an audit). Write 8 optimized hero headline candidates (one line each, ASCII, no em/en dashes, no overclaim that the AI is 'secure'/'safe'), each with a 1-line subhead, then rank them and pick the single best + 2 alternates. Cite 2-3 real 2026 examples of crisp on-demand / no-lead-time / self-serve security or devtools positioning (URLs).
